coredata.dc:title: In-situ quantification of lipids in live cells through imaging approaches
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yusi
Zhang, Rui-Qiao
Liu, Shu-Lin

coredata.dc:description: Lipids are important molecules that are widely distributed within the cell, and they play a crucial role in several biological processes such as cell membrane formation, signaling, cell motility and division. Monitoring the spatiotemporal dynamics of cellular lipids in real-time and quantifying their concentrations in situ is crucial since the local concentration of lipids initiates various signaling pathways that regulate cellular processes. In this review, we first introduced the historical background of lipid quantification methods. We then delve into the current state of the art of in situ lipid quantification, including the establishment and utility of fluorescence imaging techniques based on sensors of lipid-binding domains labeled with organic dyes or fluorescent proteins, and Raman and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) techniques that do not require lipid labeling. Next, we highlighted the biological applications of live-cell lipid quantification techniques in the study of in situ lipid distribution, lipid transformation, and lipid-mediated signaling pathways. Finally, we discussed the technical challenges and prospects for the development of lipid quantification in live cells, with the aim of promoting the development of in situ lipid quantification in live cells, which may have a profound impact on the biological and medical fields.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Timeline of key developments in lipid quantification.

Structures of fluorescent dyes and the labeling reactions used for lipid labeling. R represents lipidic anchors, other functional groups or polypeptide chains. (A) The basic structures of Cy3 and Rhodamine. (B) Basic structures for Nitrobenzoxadiazole (NBD) and Boron-dibromoethylene (BODIPY). (C) Structures for Nile Red, Nile Red 3 (NR3) and 2-(dimethylamino)-6-acryloylnaphthalene (DAN). (D–F) Examples of “click reactions” for dye labeling. CuAAC: Copper(I)-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition; SPAAC: Strain-Promoted Alkyne-Azide Cycloaddition; SPIEDAC: Strain-Promoted Inverse Electron-Demand Diels-Alder Cycloaddition. (G) Alkylation reactions for NR3 and DAN labeling with cysteine residue. (H) Chemical structures of cholesterol, dehydroergosterol and cholestatrienol. (I) Chemical structures of FR0, WCR, WCY and WCB.

Solvatochromic-dye-labeled lipid-binding protein-based sensor. (A) Screening of lipid-binding domain. (B) Specificity for target lipids. (C) Ratiometric lipid sensor using solvatochromic dyes on lipid-binding proteins. (D) Response of the sensor for vesicles containing various levels of lipids. (E) Giant unilamellar vesicle (GUV) calibration of the sensor. (F) GUV calibration results showing fluorescence ratio vs. lipid mole-%. (G) Live-cell imaging of spatio-temporal changes in fluorescence ratios in the plasma membrane. (H), Quantification of the angular profile in (G). Summarized from (Cho et al., 2017; Liu et al. 2014, 2017, 2018a; Zhang et al., 2018).

Fluorescence resonance-energy-transfer biosensors of phosphatidic acid (PA). Schematic of a PA monitor (Pii). K-Ras4B-CT indicates the C-terminal region of K-Ras4B. The CFP/YFP pair demonstrated a paralleled conformation and exhibited FRET when the sensor was unbound to PA and detached from the IPM. However, when PA was generated by phospholipase D or DAG kinase in the IPM, the DOCK-2 attached to the IPM, causing the CFP/YFP pair to adopt an unparalleled conformation, resulting in a decreased FRET signal. (Nishioka et al., 2010). Copyright 2010 Elsevier.

(A) Spontaneous Raman scattering. When pump beam (ωP) interact with the molecule, it can be either elastically scattered (Rayleigh scattering) or inelastically scattered (Raman scattering) and generated a red-shifted signal (ωS). (B) Coherent Raman scattering (CRS). The pump beam (ωP) and Stokes beam (ωS) together excite a specific vibration, resulting in a Stimulated Raman Loss (SRL) of ωP and a Stimulated Raman Gain (SRG) of ωS. In addition, the excited molecule also generates a coherent anti-Stokes Raman scattering (CARS) that has stronger frequency than the pump beam. (C) Energy-level diagrams for spontaneous Raman, CARS and stimulated Raman scattering (SRS). In spontaneous Raman, the ωP excited the molecule from ground state to virtual state which then relax to the vibrational state and producing a red-shifted signal (ωS). The energy difference Ω = ωP - ωS coordinate with the molecular vibrations. In CARS, two laser beams, ωP and ωS, incident upon the molecule, when difference of the two lasers coordinates with the vibrational frequency (Ω), the molecule is resonantly excited which further producing an anti-Stokes frequency ωaS = ωP + Ω. In SRS, the coherent interaction with the molecule stimulated the emission from the virtual state of the molecule to its vibrational state, leading to a SRG of the ωS. (D) ωS, ωP and Ω in SRS and CARS, SRL on the pump pulse, and SRG on the Stokes pulse. Summarized from (Krafft et al., 2016; Gupta et al., 2019).

Multiplex coherent anti-Stokes Raman scattering (CARS) spectral imaging of adipocyte. (A) Bright-field image of an adipocyte incubated with 1:3 (mol/mol) unsaturated to saturated fatty acids. The marked region was imaged with multiplex CARS microscopy and the local lipid integrated intensity is shown in (D). Spontaneous Raman-like spectra at locations in (D) are shown in (B) for CC-stretch and (C) CH stretch regions. Mapping of acyl chain unsaturation (E) and acyl chain order (F) in D, based on acquired spectra (Rinia et al., 2008). Copyright 2008 Elsevier.

Various distributions of neutral lipids in mammalian cells visualized with hyperspectral stimulated Raman scattering (SRS). (A, B) Images of cultured mouse macrophage cells RAW 264.7 (A) and rat hepatic cells McA-RH7777 (B). Maximum intensity projection of 20-frame SRS images over 2825–3050 cm−1 shows the overall cell morphology. R3015/2965 images reveal storage regions of cholesteryl ester (CE) in macrophage cells and triacylglycerol (TAG) in hepatic cells. Total lipid levels were visualized by imaging 2850-cm−1 H–C–H bonds for all fatty acyl chains. Scale bar: 10 μm. (C, D) Averaged spectra of lipid domains in macrophage cells (C) and hepatic cells (D) closely resemble those of CE and TAG, respectively. (E) Macrophage and hepatic cells contain two classes of neutral lipid molecules with R3015/2965 peaks at 0.4 and 0.65, respectively (Fu et al., 2014). Copyright 2014 American Chemical Society.

(A) Akt isoforms was autoinhibited by the N-terminal pleckstrin homology (PH) domain. (B) Class I PI3K. activation leads to formation of PI(3,4,5)P3, which is then converted to PI(3,4)P2 by SHIP2. (C) Akt1 and Akt3 interact with PI(3,4,5)P3 and Akt2 binds with PI(3,4)P2 to recruit it to the plasma membrane (PM). (D) Selective PM recruitment of Akt isoforms by PI(3,4,5)P3 and PI(3,4)P2 leads to immediate activation of Akt1 and Akt2 by mTORC2. PDK1 and mTORC2 are also reported to be recruited to the PM. (E) PI(3,4)P2 is selectively enriched in vesicles and is transferred to early endosomes (EE) via endocytosis. (F) Akt2 is selectively recruited to EEs by the endosomal pool of PI(3,4)P2。 (G) Endosome-activated Akt2 may play a key role in phosphorylating and regulating GSK3. (H) Endosomal PI(3,4)P2 is converted to PI3P by INPP4B, terminating Akt2 activation (Liu et al., 2018a). Copyright 2018 Elsevier.

head.ce:title: In-situ quantification of lipids in live cells through imaging approaches

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lipids are important molecules that are widely distributed within the cell, and they play a crucial role in several biological processes such as cell membrane formation, signaling, cell motility and division. Monitoring the spatiotemporal dynamics of cellular lipids in real-time and quantifying their concentrations in situ is crucial since the local concentration of lipids initiates various signaling pathways that regulate cellular processes. In this review, we first introduced the historical background of lipid quantification methods. We then delve into the current state of the art of in situ lipid quantification, including the establishment and utility of fluorescence imaging techniques based on sensors of lipid-binding domains labeled with organic dyes or fluorescent proteins, and Raman and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) techniques that do not require lipid labeling. Next, we highlighted the biological applications of live-cell lipid quantification techniques in the study of in situ lipid distribution, lipid transformation, and lipid-mediated signaling pathways. Finally, we discussed the technical challenges and prospects for the development of lipid quantification in live cells, with the aim of promoting the development of in situ lipid quantification in live cells, which may have a profound impact on the biological and medical fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lipids are a vital class of biomolecules comprised of long-chain fatty acids and other components such as cholesterol, phospholipids, and sphingolipids. They play a crucial role in several biological processes, including cell membrane formation, energy storage, and participation in biological signaling pathways (Harayama and Riezman, 2018; Storck et al., 2018; van Meer et al., 2008). Dysregulation of lipids is linked to various pathologies, such as cardiovascular diseases associated with cholesterol dysregulation (Nordestgaard and Varbo, 2014; Rader and Hovingh, 2014; Ridker, 2014), Niemann-Pick disease attributed to sphingomyelin dysregulation (Domon et al., 2012), and some cancers associated with disorders of phosphatidylinositol (Goncalves et al., 2018; Wymann and Schneiter, 2008). Therefore, lipids can serve as biomarkers for early disease diagnosis, monitoring disease progression, and predicting treatment response. Since local concentrations of lipids also play a key role in initiating various signaling pathways that regulate cellular processes, in situ real-time monitoring of lipid concentrations is essential for understanding the mechanisms of lipid-related signaling pathways and for improving the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of lipid-related diseases.

Previously, researchers usually used chromatography-coupled mass spectrometry to detect and quantify intracellular lipid composition (Khoury et al., 2018). Although this method can provide accurate quantification and identification of lipid components, it disrupts the original spatial distribution of intracellular lipids and does not allow real-time in situ detection of the distribution of lipid molecules in live cells. To address this, researchers have developed alternatives such as fluorescent lipid analogs or fluorescent-protein-labeled lipid sensors. These methods enable the examination of the spatial distribution of specific lipid molecules within live cells, but still do not allow the quantification of changes in the local content of lipid molecules within live cells (Daemen et al., 2016; Varnai et al. 2006, 2017). Subsequently, ratiometric fluorescent lipid sensors were developed to enable in situ dynamic detection and quantitative analysis of lipid molecules in live cells. These sensors enable simultaneous in-situ lipid imaging and quantification in live cells. Additionally, to reduce the interference with the in situ spatial distribution of lipids by the introduction of exogenous probes, non-invasive label-free methods such as Raman microscopy and MRI were developed for in situ lipid imaging and quantitative analysis in live cells based on the intrinsic properties of lipids.

In this review, we first provide a brief retrospective overview of the development of methods for in situ imaging and quantitative detection of lipids in living cells, and discussed the advantages of current popular fluorescence radiometric imaging methods, Raman microscopy techniques, and magnetic resonance imaging techniques for quantitative detection of lipids in living cells. Next, we highlighted the current status of fluorescence, Raman and magnetic resonance imaging techniques, including labeling strategies, imaging methods, etc., respectively. We then introduced the latest applications of in situ quantitative detection of lipids in living cells in the biological field. Finally, we summarized and provided a perspective on the techniques with a view to their further development and wider application.

Modern lipid chemistry has its roots in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries when fat was first identified in the milky blood of animals (Olson, 1998). Later, cholesterol was discovered in gallstones and quantified using sulfuric acid and acetic acid in chloroform, a reaction now known as the Lieberman-Burchard reaction (Liebermann and Ilinski, 1885; Minovici, 1908). The first phospholipid was discovered in egg yolk and brain tissue during this time (Zeisel, 2012). Advances in separation methods, particularly chromatography, allowed for the identification of additional types of lipids in the twentieth century (Elsden, 1946; Irvine, 2016; McNamara et al., 2006; Peterson and Johnson, 1948; Zeisel, 2012). Lipid quantification methods and their timelines are shown in Fig. 1 . In the 1960s, chromatography and combinations with mass spectrometry (MS) or nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) enabled lipid separation and quantitative analysis to be performed simultaneously with high efficiency (Jungalwala and Cama, 1965; Li et al., 2017b; Ryhage and Stenhagen, 1960). However, these quantitative methods require the separation of purified lipids by chromatography, centrifugation, and other processes (Khoury et al., 2018; Saini et al., 2021), and do not provide information on the in-situ distribution of lipids in live cells.

To address this challenge, fluorescent lipid analogs were developed in the 1970s and 1980s to mimic natural lipids in the cell, and the fluorescence microscopes enabled imaging of lipid distributions (Archer, 1975; Craig et al., 1981; Stoffel and Michaelis, 1976; Szoka et al., 1981). However, these lipid analogs were not fully representative of natural lipids, and methods for in-situ detection of natural lipids in live cells were sorely needed. In the 1990s, the discovery of lipid-binding domains in proteins enabled sensors to detect natural lipids in living cells (Ferguson. et al., 1995; Sutton et al., 1995). In 1998, lipid-binding proteins were combined with fluorescent proteins to construct high-specificity fluorescent lipid sensors that enabled imaging of dynamics and distributions in live cells (Oancea et al., 1998). In 2004, these genetically encoded sensors were used in live cells to visualize and quantify lipid concentrations (Cicchetti et al., 2004). However, such sensors suffered from the disadvantages of difficult calibration, high background and low sensitivity, resulting in only semi-quantitative information being obtained. Then, over the last two decades, ratiometric lipid sensors labeled with fluorescent proteins or organic dyes were developed to better quantify lipid concentrations in live cells in a spatiotemporal manner (Hu et al., 2022; Yoon et al., 2011). These methods were used to study lipid-mediated protein function and signaling pathways (Liu et al., 2018a; Zhang et al., 2018).

Subsequently, with the development of Raman imaging techniques, Coherent Raman Scattering Microscopy (CRS) with higher imaging speeds and rates enables the non-invasive and label-free detection of lipid molecules (Syed and Smith, 2017). CRS has two imaging modalities: Coherent anti-Stokes Raman scattering (CARS) and stimulated Raman scattering (SRS) microscopy. CARS microscopy was first used for lipid imaging in 1999 (Zumbusch et al., 1999), followed by lipid quantification in 2002 (Cheng et al., 2002). SRS was used for lipid imaging in 2008 (Freudiger et al., 2008). Raman microscopy is a non-invasive, label-free detection method that can be used to quantitively image lipids in situ in live cells, particularly cholesterol and fatty acids, in terms of distributions, functions, and signaling pathways. Meanwhile, lipid quantification with MRI in tissues was also carried out according to the listed literatures (Fuchs et al., 2013; Brescia et al., 2007).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ lipid quantification via fluorescence microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence microscopy is widely used in biological research due to the availability of bright fluorophores as well as the commercial availability, affordability, and ease of operation of fluorescence microscopes. Therefore, it has been used to quantify live-cell lipids in situ. In fluorescence imaging, the selection of fluorophores strongly influences the imaging methods and the final results. The most commonly used fluorophores are organic dyes and fluorescent proteins, each with its advantages and limitations.

Small fluorescent organic dyes are extensively used in lipid quantification due to their favorable features such as high brightness, good photostability, tunable structures, facile labeling strategies, commercial availability, and wide color ranges (Huang et al., 2023). Fluorophores can be directly incorporated onto lipid molecules (Maier et al., 2002), through acyl chains, hydrophobic groups, or polar hydrophilic head groups (e.g., cyanine dyes, rhodamine dyes) (Fig. 2 A). However, large fluorophores like cyanine dyes and rhodamine are not suitable for attachment to acyl chains due to steric hindrance; instead, smaller fluorophores like nitrobenzoxadiazole (NBD) and boron-dibromoethylene (BODIPY) are commonly used (Klymchenko and Kreder, 2014) (Fig. 2B). The development of “Click reactions” have facilitated the convenience and availability of lipid labeling options (Haberkant and Holthuis, 2014) (Fig. 2D–F). One limitation of fluorophore-labeled lipids is that the labeling may change or mask the biophysical properties and functional groups of lipids (Maekawa and Fairn, 2014). Sterol analogs such as dehydroergosterol and cholestatrienol, both of which are inherently fluorescent cholesterol analogs, are also available (Maxfield and Wustner, 2012) (Fig. 2H). However, such fluorescent lipid analogs need to be embedded into the original cell membrane during live cell culture and do not accurately provide in situ spatial distribution and quantitative information on natural lipids.

Lipid-binding quantification sensors consist of a lipid-binding module and a fluorescent group, enabling specific binding to natural lipids with minimal interference to cellular functions. The specificity of the module is crucial to avoid non-specific binding, while the affinity should be low to sense lipid dynamics. Therefore, lipid-binding protein domains have become the best choice (Daemen et al., 2016). Some modifications to lipid-binding proteins, such as tandem repeats and point mutations, are sometimes necessary to improve their specificity, alter their dynamic range, enhance their stability, and introduce dye-linking sites (Cho et al., 2017). Many reports have summarized lipid-binding protein domains, their target lipids, their equilibrium dissociation constants (K d), and methods to measure K d (Hammond and Balla, 2015; Hu et al., 2022; Lemmon, 2008; Varnai et al., 2017).

To break through the limitations of single-emission fluorescent dyes in lipid-binding sensors, researchers have developed ratiometric fluorescent dyes that exhibit variation in dual- or multi-emission bands with built-in references between the emission bands for more accurate quantification (Fisher and Campbell, 2014; Lee et al., 2015b; Li et al., 2014; Park et al., 2020). Solvatochromic fluorophores have also been used for labeling lipid-binding proteins because they are sensitive to the polarity of the environment and exhibit a hypochromic shift in less polar environments such as lipid membranes and nonpolar solvents (Klymchenko, 2017; Loving et al., 2010). Examples of common solvatochromic fluorophores used in ratiometric sensors include Nile Red, NBD, and 2-(dimethylamino)-6-acryloylnaphthalene (DAN), which display significant intensity increases in blue-shifted emission spectra (Klymchenko, 2017) (Fig. 2C).

In essence, lipid-binding proteins are recruited to the membrane via nonspecific electrostatic interactions between anionic lipids and cationic amino acid residues, followed by specific hydrophobic or hydrogen-bonding interactions that result in conformational changes and membrane penetration by the protein (Cho and Stahelin, 2005). To create a ratiometric lipid sensor that displays enhanced blue-shifted fluorescence upon lipid binding, a solvatochromic dye can be introduced near the site of the lipid-binding domain.

Orthogonal dye labeling strategies are available to label proteins without disrupting their functional structure. One common method is labeling with natural amino acids, particularly cysteine (Cys), due to its low abundance and high nucleophilicity compared to other residues (Lin and Wang, 2008). Small fluorescent molecules can bind to the thiol group in the side chain via alkylation reactions or disulfide bond exchanges (Fig. 2G). The use of Cys is advantageous because it can be transformed into other amino acids, or placed at the desired site without affecting the protein's functionality and stability. It is important to note that, labeling lipid-binding proteins involves only one exposed Cys, which existed naturally in the desired location or was transformed from other amino acids. Remaining exposed Cys residues must be transformed to eliminate undesired labeling. However, if the protein's function relies on a Cys residue, its transformation may interfere with lipid binding. In such cases, synthesized amino acids with azide or tetrazine groups can serve as alternatives for site-specific labeling with fluorophores containing cyclooctyne or trans-cyclooctene, or vice versa (Zhang et al., 2015). Nevertheless, labeling natural amino acids is still preferred due to the laborious and costly nature of labeling synthesized amino acids.

Liu et al. developed a cholesterol sensor using the domain 4 of cholesterol-specific binding protein perfringolysin O (PFO-D4) (Liu et al., 2017; Ramachandran et al., 2002; Shimada et al., 2002; Tweten, 2005). The researchers introduced several mutations to make the protein domain more suitable for a lipid sensor. D434A decreased the anionic charge to increase electrostatic interactions with the lipid, Y415A strengthened the protein stability, and Q433W, D434W, and A463W increased membrane permeability. The solvatochromic dye was labeled at C459. These modifications resulted in different cholesterol affinity and detection ranges. This set of cholesterol sensors was designed to cover a large range of cholesterol concentrations. The modified lipid domain labeled with NR3 or DAN allowed simultaneous imaging of cholesterol on the inner plasma membrane (IPM) and outer plasma membrane (OPM), respectively. The NR3 fluorescence emission increased and was blue-shifted from the red channel (FR) to an orange channel (FO). The increased DAN fluorescence emission blue-shifted from the green channel (FG) to a blue channel (FB). Because the blue-shifted channels always exhibited increased intensity, they generated in-vitro calibration curves for cholesterol concentrations in giant unilamellar vesicles based on the FO/FR ratio for NR3 and the FB/FG ratio for DAN. The calibration curves exhibited a cholesterol detection range of 0–40 mol%. The membrane FO/FR and FB/FG values were determined after exposing the sensors to live cells, and the corresponding cholesterol concentration was found on the calibration curves, as summarized in Fig. 3 .

The solvatochromic fluorophore library is limited and often suffers from low photostability, weak solvatochromism, and low water solubility (Klymchenko, 2017). To compensate, Sharma et al. addressed these limitations by using FR0, a dye synthesized by themselves which demonstrated improved brightness, solvatochromism, and photostability (Kucherak et al., 2010). Furthermore, they synthesized WCY, WCB, and WCR by transforming the formyl group of FR0 to an acrylohydrazone, an acryloyloxime, and a vinyl ketone, respectively. These novel fluorophores exhibited high quantum yields, increased brightness and photostability, and narrower emission bands. Among these, WCB and WCR were particularly effective for high-signal-to-noise-ratio ratiometric imaging, making them ideal for use in lipid sensors (Sharma et al., 2020) (Fig. 2I).

Fluorescent protein (FP)-labeled lipid sensors offer several advantages over organic dye-labeled sensors. In FP-labeled sensors, the lipid-binding domains are similar to those in organic dye-labeled sensors, while the fluorophores are replaced with FP that can be genetically encoded and directly expressed in cells (Daemen et al., 2016). This approach allows for the use of mammalian cells to express complex proteins, resulting in highly biocompatible sensors with little toxicity. For example, the green fluorescent protein (GFP)-labeled pleckstrin homology (PH) domain of phospholipase Cδ (PLCδ) has been utilized to specifically detect the subcellular localization of phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate (PI(4,5)P2) with high specificity (Stephen A. Watt et al., 2002). FP-labeled lipid sensors have been widely used to study lipid distributions and dynamics, making them a powerful tool in lipid research.

GFP was discovered in the 1960s (Zimmer, 2009), and the emission spectra of FPs now cover the visible and near-infrared regions (Sample et al., 2009; Shcherbakova et al., 2018). However, the chromophore of FPs is well-protected within the β-barrel structure, making it unsuitable for solvatochromic sensing. To address this issue, FP can be used in two different ways: by excitation at two different wavelengths and collection of emission at a single fixed wavelength, or vice versa. The former approach can be achieved with a single FP, such as circularly permuted GFP used for sensing Ca2+ (Takeharu Nagai et al., 2001). The latter approach mostly involves two FPs and is based on fluorescence resonance-energy-transfer-(FRET) mechanism. Proper selection of FP pairs and sensor design is required for FRET, which requires that donor and acceptor FPs be in close proximity (10–100 Å), with the acceptor absorption overlapping the donor fluorescence, and the donor and acceptor transition-dipole moments must be approximately parallel (Okumoto et al., 2012). Due to the strong effects of orientation and distance on FRET, FP FRET sensor exhibit high sensitivity and rapid response time for lipid quantification in live cells.

Several factors must be considered during FP design and implementation. For example, the polypeptide chains and some FPs in genetically encoded sensors can be highly sensitive to changes in pH and ionic strength, which may result from various cellular events such as ion influxes and solute addition (Awaji et al., 2001; Griesbeck et al., 2001; Hires et al., 2008; Jayaraman et al., 2000; Kneen et al., 1998; Llopis et al., 1998). Furthermore, controlling the expression level of the sensor may be difficult as it may contain native sequences that are directly expressed in the cell, and it may interfere with other proteins and pathways (Hasan et al., 2004). Overexpressed sensors may inhibit signal transduction of the analyte or alter the distribution of other substrates (Kindt et al., 2007; Rich and Karpen, 2002; Tian et al., 2009). The effects of pH and ionic strength changes should be tested before applications. If no alternative proteins are available, a proper control experiment and even affinity mutations should be performed. To reduce interference from native sequences, heterologous proteins or less reactive proteins may be used, or FPs may be avoided altogether (Okumoto et al., 2012).

Cyan (CFP) and yellow fluorescence protein (YFP) are the most commonly used FP FRET pairs, with many variants have been developed, including CyPet (Nguyen and Daugherty, 2005), mCerulean3 (Markwardt et al., 2011), mTurquoise (Goedhart et al., 2010), and Cerulean (Markwardt et al., 2011) FRET donors, as well as YPet (Nguyen and Daugherty, 2005), mVenus (Nagai et al., 2002), and mCitrine (Griesbeck et al., 2001) acceptors. Other FP FRET pairs have also been utilized, such as TagRFP/mPlum (Grant et al., 2008), mOrange/mCherry (Piljic and Schultz, 2008), TagGFP/TagRFP (Shcherbo et al., 2009), Sapphire/red fluorescence protein (RFP) (Niino et al., 2009), GFP/mCherry (Tramier et al., 2006), mAmetrine/tdTomato (Ai et al., 2008) and MiCy/mKO (Karasawa et al., 2004).

In vitro experiments with genetically encoded sensors are challenging, making it difficult to obtain a lipid concentration calibration curve for the FP FRET signal. Consequently, these sensors provide relative concentrations. For example, Nishioka et al. used a Pii FRET sensor to detect phosphatidic acid (PA) levels in situ. The sensor comprised a CFP, a PA binding domain (DOCK-2), a glycine linker, a YFP, and the C-terminal of K-Ras4B (K-Ras4B-CT) to recruit in the IPM (Fig. 4 ) (Nishioka et al., 2010). The CFP/YFP pair demonstrated a parallel conformation and exhibited FRET when the sensor was unbound to PA and detached from the IPM. However, when PA was generated by phospholipase D or DAG kinase in the IPM, the DOCK-2 attached to the IPM, caused the CFP/YFP pair to adopt an unparalleled conformation, resulting in a decreased FRET signal. Leveraging this inverse correlation between PA concentration and FRET value, they successfully detected changes in PA levels in the IPM. Hertel et al. designed a Lyn-PlcR PI(4,5)P2 sensor consisting of a Lyn motif to anchor to the IPM, an enhanced YFP (eYFP), a PH domain of PLCδ1 to recognize PI(4,5)P2 in the IPM, a pseudo-ligand (PL) and an enhanced CFP (eCFP) (Hertel et al., 2020). The PLCδ1 interacted with the PI(4,5)P2 in the IPM, leading to a high FRET yield conformation of eCFP and eYFP. The FRET efficiency reduced when the negatively charged PL substituted its binding with the PLCδ1 upon PI(4,5)P2 depletion. They also developed an intensity-based single-color sensor that utilized dimerization-dependent GFP (ddGFP), which increased the emission intensity when two FPs moved into close proximity (Alford et al., 2012). The new Lyn-dPlcR sensor replaced the FRET pair in PlcR with ddGFP-A and ddFP-B, resulting in high fluorescence intensity with PI(4,5)P2 generation and low intensity with PI(4,5)P2 depletion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ lipid quantification via Coherent Raman Scattering Microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent lipid sensors have enabled high-resolution and fast acquisition for lipid imaging and quantification. However, labeled lipid sensors, whether delivered or overexpressed, may still affect the cellular lipid distribution and some organic dyes may exhibit nonspecific binding, which can be a limitation (Yen et al., 2010). Thus, it is advantageous to develop label-free in-situ approaches to quantify lipids in live cells. Raman-based label-free microscopy is not affected by photobleaching and enables highly multiplexed imaging using narrow Raman peaks (Syed and Smith, 2017).

Over the last three decades, Raman microscopy has undergone significant improvements, enabling efficient sample analysis (Carey, 1999). Although early micro-Raman spectroscopy had low resolution and long acquisition time (Abraham and Etz, 1979) (Fig. 5 A), recent advancements in laser sources, microscopes, detectors, and signal processing techniques have revolutionized Raman microscopy. In particular, phase-locked pulsed lasers have enhanced Raman transition rates by more than a million fold (Min et al., 2011), resulting in coherent Raman scattering (CRS), which has enabled high-quality imaging and quantitative analysis of biological samples under a second (Krafft et al., 2016). Consequently, CRS techniques have been successfully applied to in vitro lipid imaging and quantification, providing valuable insights into cellular lipid metabolism and distribution (Gupta et al., 2019).

CRS microscopy is a label-free non-invasive method for analyzing chemical compositions of biological samples by detecting characteristic molecular vibrations, particularly those strong modes associated with CH2 groups. To selectively image lipids, the asymmetric C–H stretching mode at 2845-cm−1 is often utilized. CRS is induced by irradiating the sample with two laser beams, a pump laser and a Stokes laser, with respective frequencies ωp and ωs. When the frequency difference is equal to a specific vibrational frequency of the target molecule (Ω = ωp−ωs), the Raman scattering cross-section is resonantly enhanced, resulting in a strong CRS signal (Fig. 5B).

CARS and SRS are two types of CRS imaging techniques that allow for non-invasive label-free analysis of biological samples. In CARS, the molecular vibrations are coherently driven when Ω interacts with the pump beam, resulting in an enhanced anti-Stokes Raman signal (ωaS = Ω+ωp). On the other hand, SRS occurs when there is an excess of pump or Stokes photons in the excitation beam, amplifying the strongest mode in the Raman spectrum, while suppressing all other Raman modes (Fig. 5C and D). As both CARS and SRS are nonlinear optical processes that generate signals only in the focal plane of the objective, they allow for intrinsic three-dimensional slicing by scanning in the X, Y and Z axes. Both CARS and SRS are diffraction-limited techniques for subcellular imaging, with resolutions down to a few hundred nanometers. The SRS signal is linear relative to the concentration of the resonant vibration, whereas the CARS signal is quadratic. Moreover, SRS also has a higher signal-to-noise as there is no non-resonant background; however, the instrumentation is more complex (Gupta et al., 2019; Parodi et al., 2020).

Accurate quantification of lipid content from CARS intensity requires processing the CARS signal, which increases quadratically with molecular vibrational concentration. Chen et al. developed an automated image-analysis algorithm based on a maximum entropy threshold to monitor lipid accumulation and hydrolysis in macrophages (Chen et al., 2013). Rinia et al. used a spectral analysis tool in combination with multiplex CARS to retrieve spontaneous Raman-like spectra that were linearly related to the number of vibrating molecules (Rinia et al., 2008). They generated high-resolution spectral images that revealed the physical state and chemical compositions of individual lipid droplets in adipocytes treated with various components of exogenous free fatty acids, as shown in Fig. 6 .

The linear dependence of the SRS signal with the number of vibrating molecules enables direct quantification, making it a valuable tool for monitoring biological processes. For instance, Freudiger et al. imaged the uptake and metabolism of unsaturated free fatty acids by detecting the 2845-cm−1 CH2 stretching vibration and the 3015-cm−1 CC vibration using SRS (Freudiger et al., 2008). Similarly, Wang et al. utilized 2845-cm−1 CH2 SRS to image lipid storage, and the average SRS intensity was used to quantify the storage capacity (Wang et al., 2011). These studies demonstrate the potential of SRS as a quantitative imaging tool for studying lipid metabolism and storage in live cells.

Both CARS and SRS lack sufficient chemical specificity to distinguish multiple biomolecules in complex cellular environments. Hyperspectral SRS, however, is a variant of SRS that overcomes this limitation by mapping the entire vibrational spectrum of a sample through the use of laser pulses with tuned wavelengths, This generates a stack of SRS images at a series of different frequencies, providing more comprehensive information about the chemical composition and distribution of the sample (Beier et al., 2011; Ravn et al., 2011; Xu et al., 2023). Unlike conventional SRS, which only probes a single vibrational spectrum at a time, hyperspectral SRS offers higher accuracy and spectral resolution, making it a valuable tool for studying lipid quantification and distribution in live cells. Because of its ability to provide accurate information with high spatial and spectral resolution, hyperspectral SRS is widely applied in studying lipid quantification and distributions in live cells. In particular, it has been used to accurately analyze cellular lipid distributions following changes in cellular metabolic states and can be used in combination with spectral-analysis tools to extract Raman spectra of different molecules from convoluted SRS spectra (Fu et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2013). For example, Oh et al. imaged the Raman peak at 2853 cm−1 which originates from –CH2- in fatty acids (Oh et al., 2022), while Li et al. monitored the 3002 cm−1 characteristic peak of CC–H in unsaturated lipids and the 2900 cm−1 C–H peak observed in all lipids using the SRS intensity ratio (I3002/I2900) to reflect lipid unsaturation levels (Li et al., 2017a). Deuterated cholesterol (D38-cholesterol) was used as a probe to map heterogeneity in cholesterol distribution and enable a better understanding of its metabolism, as it exhibits an enhanced C−D stretching peak at 2100 cm−1, unlike C–H in natural cholesterol at 2700–3100 cm−1 (Alfonso-Garcia et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ lipid quantification by magnetic resonance imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Nuclear magnetic resonance has been the subject of several Nobel Prizes, including Chemistry (Ernst in 1991 and Ussrich in 2002), Physics (Rabi in 1944, Bloch and Purcell in 1952) and Medicine (Lauterbur and Mansfield in 2003) (Borisjuk et al., 2013). Although MRI was first used to image the human torso in 1977, the scanning time was several hours and the resolution was only 0.7 cm (Damadian et al., 1977). However, with the advances in technology, current MRI instruments provide much better resolution in just a few seconds. MRI is widely used in the clinical diagnosis of cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases, sports-related injuries, organ diseases in the thoracic and abdominal cavities, and the evaluation and tracking of tumors.

All atoms contain a nucleus, which is a positively charged particle. Some nuclei, such as 1H, 13C and 31P, are able to spin and thus generate a local magnetic field whose direction follows the Ampere's Law. Among these nuclei, 1H is mostly measured and detected in clinical and chemical experiments as it is abundant and has only two energy states: the lower energy state (the spin is aligned with the magnetic field) and the higher energy state (the spin is not aligned with the magnetic field). Without the external magnetic field, all nuclei direct randomly and show no magnetic performance. When a strong external magnetic field (B0) is applied, their magnetic field aligns along the direction of the B0 to generate Larmor precession. Then, a series of radio frequency (RF) current pulse is applied at an angle (usually perpendicular) to the B0. When the RF current pulse is tuned to the frequency at which the 1H resonates, the 1H will transit from higher energy state to the lower energy state. When the RF current pulse is turned off, the 1H releases the energy and switches from the lower energy state to the higher energy state. During this process, the energy they emitted can be detected by the MRI instrument, and each 1H will resonate at a characteristic frequency depending on the environment and the chemical properties of the molecules (Grover et al., 2015).

Although studies of lipid quantification with MRI have been reported, due to its limited resolution, typically ranging from a few to a hundred micrometers, lipid quantification with MRI is currently mostly confined to live tissue imaging. For example, Bargalló et al. employed MRI to detect lipid core in carotid plaques. Their study revealed an intriguing finding: an inverse relationship between the increasing proportion of omega-3 eicosapentaenoic acid in red blood cells and the plaque lipid burden (Bargallo et al., 2017). Seifert et al. directly quantified myelin through two soli-state MRI techniques, ultra-short echo time MRI and zero-echo time MRI, in the ovine spinal cords (Seifert et al., 2017). Furthermore, there are also applications of lipid storage detection in plant seeds, such as cotton seeds (Horn et al., 2012), tobacco seeds (Fuchs et al., 2013), and olives (Brescia et al., 2007).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of in-situ lipid quantification in live cells
Mapping lipid distributions in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: Lipids are intricately distributed throughout the cytoplasm via complex mechanisms, affecting both cell structure and various regulatory and functional processes (Murate and Kobayashi, 2016; van Meer, 2011). Thus, lipid distributions can reveal mechanisms and functions.

For example, Liu et al. utilized a PFO-D4-based cholesterol sensor labeled with solvatochromic dyes to measure cholesterol concentrations in live-cell IPMs and OPMs simultaneously (Liu et al., 2017). By adjusting the detection range with different mutations in the labeled lipid-binding domains labeled for IPM and OPM cholesterol concentrations, they found that the OPM had twelve times more cholesterol than the IPM, maintained by the ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporters ABCA1 and ABCG1 that moved cholesterol from the IPM to the OPM. This unsymmetrical distribution has a significantly impact on cellular signaling pathways; for instance, the Wnt pathway is triggered by IPM cholesterol.

Similarly, Nishioka et al. employed FRET-based sensors consisting of a CFP, a lipid-binding structural domain, a YFP, and K-Ras4B-CT for phospholipids detection. The K-Ras4B-CT enabled the sensor to anchor in the IPM (Nishioka et al., 2008), where it exhibited strong FRET when the lipid-binding structural domain interacted with the lipids, and low FRET when there was no interaction. By introducing three different lipid-binding domains (PLCδ-PH, PKCβII-C1 and FAPP1-PH) for PI(4,5)P2, DAG, and PI4P, respectively, they transfected all the sensors into Madin-Darby canine kidney (MDCK) cells and mapped various phospholipid concentrations under different stimuli. In migrating cells, the phospholipids exhibited higher concentrations in the migration direction and a higher turnover rate.

Moreover, Fu et al. used hyperspectral SRS to simultaneously quantify cholesteryl ester (CE) and triacylglycerol (TAG) (Fu et al., 2014). They utilized the I3015/2965 intensity ratio to quantitatively map the two neutral lipids in live cells, as the TAG spectrum was stronger than that for CE at 3015 cm−1, but weaker at 2965 cm−1 (Fig. 7 ). In mouse liver cells, after injection of tunicamycin, lipid distribution expansion was attributed to TAG accumulations. Furthermore, they simultaneously mapped deuterated saturated and unsaturated fatty acids in C. elegans, which revealed that the metabolism of saturated and unsaturated fatty acids can be independent of each other, in contrast to the research done by Listenberger et al., (2003).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting lipid conversions in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: To gain a better understanding of lipid regulation and behaviors, it is necessary to detect the conversion dynamics and locations of different lipids, even though they can be readily interconverted by enzymatic catalysis despite their structural similarity (van Meer et al., 2008). Simultaneous in-situ quantification of dual lipids in live cells is a way to realize this goal.

In-situ quantification of PI(4,5)P2 and phosphatidylinositol 3,4,5-trisphosphate (PI(3,4,5)P3) can be performed simultaneously by using two labeled orthogonal fluorescent sensors, an engineered PH domain of myosin X (eMyoXPH) labeled with NR3 and an engineered ENTH domain (eENTH) labeled with DAN (DAN-eENTH) (Liu et al., 2014). After insulin stimulation, PI(4,5)P2 was converted to PI(3,4,5)P3, and time-lapse imaging showed a high degree of spatial correlation between their increase and decrease, respectively. Quantitative analysis revealed that the ratio PI(3,4,5)P3/PI(4,5)P2 increased from 0.25 ± 0.05 to 0.5 after 5 min of stimulation and then returned to the initial level. The ratio also increased in PTEN-deficient human prostate cancer cells upon insulin stimulation, demonstrating the robustness of these orthogonal sensors for lipid quantification in a spatiotemporally resolved manner in living cells. Hertel et al. used ddFPs to construct Lyn-dPlcR and Lyn-dInPAkt sensors that emitted strong green or red fluorescence when interacting with PI(4,5)P2 or PI(3,4,5)P3, respectively (Hertel et al., 2020). They transfected the sensors into Cos7 cells and observed their conversion after exposure to epidermal growth factor (EGF). The decrease in dInPAkt fluorescence intensity and the increase in dPlcR indicated the conversion of PI(4,5)P2 to PI(3,4,5)P3 in the IPM following EGF stimulation.

There are various methods to investigate lipid signaling activities, including western blotting, real-time polymerase chain reactions and single-particle tracking. However, spatiotemporal visualization of lipid concentrations provides valuable insights into lipid-associated signaling pathways.

For example, Zhang et al. utilized PFO-D4-DAN and YDA-WCRX sensors to determine cholesterol concentrations in the OPM and IPM, respectively, to elucidate the mechanism by which PTCH1 regulates Smoothened (SMO) activation. Their findings revealed that PTCH1, a major receptor for mammalian Hedgehog signals, transports cholesterol from IPM to the OPM, maintaining a low cholesterol concentration in the IPM and thereby keeping SMO inactive (Zhang et al., 2018). However, upon binding to ShhN, PTCH1 blocks cholesterol transport, allowing for its accumulation in the IPM and subsequent activation of SMO.

The study by Liu et al. utilized the DAN-eMyoX-PH and NR3-eTapp1-cPH dual lipid sensors to detect PI(3,4,5)P3 and phosphatidylinositol 3,4-bisphosphate (PI(3,4)P2), respectively (Liu et al., 2018a). The results showed that after platelet-derived growth factor exposure, PI(3,4,5)P3 activated Akt1 and Akt3 in the IPM, while PI(3,4)P2 activated Akt2 in the IPM and early endosomes. Moreover, the enrichment of PI(3,4)P2 in vesicles and its transfer to the endosomes recruited Akt2 to the endosomes, where it subsequently activated GSK3 (Fig. 8 ).

Le et al. investigated the heterogeneity of LD formation rates during 3T3-L1 differentiation, using FP labeling, flow cytometry, and CARS microscopy (Le and Cheng, 2009). They found that the insulin signaling cascade, which regulates insulin sensitivity, glucose uptake, kinase activity, and insulin degradation rate, also affects the differentiation of 3T3-L1 cells into adipocytes. Single-cell fluorescence microscopy and CARS microscopy were used to determine the origin of drug-induced phenotypic changes in adipogenesis by insulin signaling cascade dynamics.

In-situ lipid quantification is a crucial aspect of understanding lipid content, dynamics, and behavior in living cells, providing insights into lipid metabolism, conversions, relocations, and signaling pathways. In this review, we present several commonly used techniques for quantifying in-situ lipid concentrations in live cells, including solvatochromic dye- or FP- labeled sensors for fluorescence microscopy imaging, and label-free imaging via Raman microcopy or MRI. Fluorescent imaging is a widely utilized method for lipid quantification due to its ease of preparation and ability to cover a broad range of lipid types, particularly phospholipids. FP-labeled sensors are commonly used for quantifying lipids inside the cells, while dye-labeled sensors are employed for the OPM. Additionally, Raman imaging and MRI serve as powerful tools for non-invasive lipid quantification, with particular relevance for fatty acid, TAG and cholesterol. MRI also holds immense significance in clinical applications. Nevertheless, applications of lipid quantification techniques remain limited because the technology requires a deep understanding of chemical, biological, and physical aspects, requiring either generalists or extensive cooperation. In the following, we have several suggestions to make these techniques more available and impactful.

Current methods for lipid quantification fluorescence imaging in live cells largely rely on fluorescent proteins and organic dyes, which have limited emission band widths, brightness, and photostability, hindering long-term, multicolor imaging. Also, dye-labeled, lipid-binding domain-based sensors exhibit high specificity and have a wide range of applications due to the availability of sensor design and modifications, yet suffers from non-specific binding of the dyes. Genetically encoded FRET-based FP sensors are readily available but have a lower dynamic range and photostability, and cannot detect lipids in the OPM, making them less applicable for quantification. Additionally, Label-free Raman microcopy enables lipid quantification with high rates and resolution, but its application is limited to detecting C–H bonds or CC–H bonds on acyl chains, which cannot specifically identify phospholipids. Thus, Raman microscopy is a limited by the range of appropriate sample. MRI is widely used for clinical diagnosis of patients and cellular-level plant samples, but the micron-level resolution severely hinders its single-cell applications. Here, we summarized the pros and cons of each method to aid researchers in understanding their advantages and limitations before applying them (Table 1 ).

Quantum dots (QDs), a type of nanoparticle, have been suggested as a promising alternative due to their outstanding optical properties (Bigdeli et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2018). Unlike organic dyes and fluorescent proteins which have small Stokes shifts (<25 nm) and may cause self-quenching and fluorescence detection errors (Fan et al., 2013), QDs have outstanding optical properties with narrow fluorescence emission spectra and broad excitation spectra that enable multiple fluorophore excitations. Additionally, they can be water-soluble, biocompatible, chemically inert, and cost-effective (Resch-Genger et al., 2008). Therefore, the utilization of QDs as lipid sensors for in-situ quantification imaging is a promising alternative to traditional methods.

Fluorescence ratiometric nanosensors offer either one reference fluorescence signal or two reversible fluorescence signals. The former could use optically stable nanoparticles as the target-insensitive reference signal to standardize the target-sensitive signal. The latter requires two target-sensitive fluorescent signals (Huang et al., 2018). Ratiometric nanosensor mechanisms include photoinduced electron transfer, intramolecular charge transfer, and FRET (Bigdeli et al., 2019). They have been used to detect heavy-metal ions such as Hg2+ (Liu et al., 2018b; Mu et al., 2014), Cu2+ (Cai et al., 2018; Zou et al., 2016), Fe3+ (Wang et al., 2016a; Wu et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2018), Cd2+ (Qian et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2016b), Pb2+ (Kuo et al., 2015), small molecules such as dopamine (Farahmand Nejad and Hormozi-Nezhad, 2017), melamine (Qu et al., 2017), trinitrotoluene (Lu et al., 2017; Xu and Lu, 2015; Zou et al., 2011), glucose (Hao et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2014; Yu et al., 2017), and glutathione (Gui et al., 2012), different pH values (Ma et al., 2015; Pan et al., 2016; Shi et al., 2012), reactive oxygen species such as hydroxyl radicals (Liu et al. 2016a, 2016b; Zhuang et al., 2014) and hydrogen peroxide (Du et al., 2014; Li et al., 2017c; Ma et al., 2017), biomolecules such as proteins (Chang et al., 2016; Lee et al., 2015a; Tyrakowski and Snee, 2014), enzymes (Gill et al., 2008; Sidhu et al., 2017; Yan et al., 2015), and nucleic acids (Del Bonis-O'Donnell et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2018), and conditions such as temperature (McLaurin et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2013). However, no lipid detection has been reported by using ratiometric nanosensors. Lipids are crucial signal messengers that indirectly interact with protein or other lipids via lipid-lipid or lipid-protein interactions. Lipid-binding domains undergo conformational changes when binding lipids, which can induce fluorescence changes if the domain was coordinated with nanoparticles. Thus, combining nanosensors with lipid binding domains may work for in-situ lipid detection and quantification.

Expanding lipid detection to actual patient samples is crucial for disease prevention and early diagnoses. Current in-situ lipid quantification techniques have mainly focused on investigating lipid distributions (Iyoshi et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2014; Sharma et al., 2020), or signaling pathways (Liu et al., 2018a), but efforts could be directed towards diseases associated with lipid dysregulation (Butler et al., 2020). For example, certain types of cancer have upregulated PIs, particularly PI(3,4,5)P3, due to disordered PI metabolisms through the hyperactivation of the phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (PI3K) signaling pathway (Goncalves et al., 2018; Ijuin, 2019; Wymann and Schneiter, 2008). This PI(3,4,5)P3 accumulation could lead to hyperactivation of other related kinases, such as Akt isoforms, which induce cell proliferation, anti-apoptosis, cell migration, and, eventually, cancer (Goncalves et al., 2018; Hoxhaj and Manning, 2020; Ijuin, 2019). Early cancer diagnosis is critical to improving survival rates and reducing side effects (Fitzgerald et al., 2022). However, as cancer detection methods become more sensitive, false positives might occur. Therefore, early cancer diagnosis involves a combination of multiple tests using various samples, such as fecal immunochemical tests (Sarkeala et al., 2021), specific antigen tests (Van Poppel et al., 2021), DNA screening (Lenaerts et al., 2021) and medical imaging (Fitzgerald et al., 2022). Cellular lipid content could be a parameter for early cancer diagnosis, and a lipid quantification assay via ratiometric sensors could be performed by measuring the content of an important lipid, such as PI(3,4,5)P3, in the PM of blood cells. With the assistance of sorting and statistical analyses, a preliminary conclusion could be drawn to improve cancer prevention.

The early diagnosis and prevention of disease are crucial for maintaining good health, but researchers are also focused on developing effective treatments for various conditions. One promising approach is to use lipid concentrations as stimuli to trigger responses in lipid-binding domains. These lipid binding domains have a tunable K d, which offers the potential for on-off drug delivery (Lin, 2020; Liu et al., 2017). For example, in PTEN-deficient cells, the PI(3,4,5)P3 concentration is higher (1.2 mol%) than that in wild-type cells (0.3 mol%) (Liu et al., 2014). Therefore, by engineering the PI(3,4,5)P3 binding domain to have an off-mode under 0.3 mol% PI(3,4,5)P3, and an on-mode above 1.2 mol-%, drugs could be specifically delivered to cancer cells. This could significantly increase the practical significance of in-situ lipid quantification, and enable wider use of such methods.
